title: Localized inhomogeneity and position-dependent stability of migratory bird formations
abstract: We investigate how localized inhomogeneity affects the geometry and stability of migratory bird formations. We use a lifting-line model with a horseshoe-vortex representation to describe the longitudinal dynamics of aerodynamic interactions. As a reference case, we first analyze homogeneous formations and show that their steady states exhibit a U-shaped geometry with hierarchical streamwise spacing, in which adjacent birds become progressively closer toward the leader. We then introduce localized inhomogeneity by modifying the wingspan of a single bird, with its physical properties determined by scaling relations. We determine the range of wingspan variation that preserves a stable formation. The stability range depends strongly on the position of the modified bird, being narrower near the outer wing and broader near the leader. These findings provide a minimal dynamical framework for understanding how local aerodynamic interactions and localized individual differences affect collective flight structures.
summary: This paper studies how individual differences in bird size affect the stability and shape of migratory flocks. It finds that the formation is most robust to changes in birds near the leader and that the leader's properties can trigger a global reorganization of the entire group.
main_problem: Investigating how localized individual differences, specifically variations in wingspan, affect the geometric structure and dynamical stability of migratory bird formations.
main_result: The stability of bird formations is position-dependent, being much broader near the leader and narrower near the outer wing; furthermore, modifying the leader causes a global reorganization of the formation, while other positions cause only localized deformation.
method: A minimal dynamical framework using a lifting-line model with a horseshoe-vortex representation, combined with linear stability analysis and numerical integration using a fourth-order Runge-Kutta method.
model_or_system: A formation of N migrating birds modeled as a system of coupled equations of motion accounting for mass, velocity, thrust, self-induced drag, and interaction-induced drag via the Biot-Savart law.
key_observables: Formation geometry (U-shaped structure, streamwise and transverse spacing), steady-state flight velocity, drag reduction rate, and stability range of wingspan variations.
important_parameters: Wingspan (b), span ratio (lambda), number of birds (N), and the spatial position of the modified bird (i).
main_assumptions: The air is treated as incompressible and inviscid; unsteady wake dynamics are neglected; aerodynamic interactions are approximated as primarily local (nearest-neighbor); and birds fly in a higher-speed regime.
figures_summary: Figure 1 defines geometric parameters; Figure 2 shows the decay of neighbor interactions; Figure 3 displays steady-state configurations, velocity increases, and drag reduction as a function of N; Figure 4 presents a stability diagram in the (i, lambda) plane; Figure 5 shows configurations for different modified bird positions.
paper_structure: The paper begins by establishing a reference homogeneous formation, moves to the introduction of localized wingspan inhomogeneity, analyzes the resulting stability and geometric response based on the position of the modified bird, and concludes with the implications for collective flight structures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Local geometric definition of adjacent birds in the formation model. For bird 𝑖, the full wingspan is 2𝑏𝑖, while the effective bound- vortex length is 2𝑎𝑖. The streamwise and transverse separations between neighboring birds are denoted by Δ𝑥and Δ𝑦, respectively. The coordinate system is defined such that the positive 𝑥direction is the flight direction and 𝑦is the transverse direction.

FIG. 2. The normalized contribution (𝐷𝑖+ 𝑗,𝑖+ 𝐷𝑖−𝑗,𝑖)/Í 𝑘≠𝑖𝐷𝑘,𝑖is shown as a function of the neighbor order 𝑗for representative birds (𝑖= 1, 4, 7), corresponding to the outermost bird, an intermediate bird on the left wing, and the leader, respectively.

FIG. 3. (a) Steady-state configurations for homogeneous forma- tions with different numbers of birds. The coordinates are scaled by the wingspan 2𝑏and shifted so that the leader is located at the origin. Different symbols denote different flock sizes, while solid and dashed lines represent the all-to-all interaction model and the nearest-neighbor approximation, respectively. (b) Dependence of the steady-state velocity on the number of birds. The inset highlights the cases corresponding to the configurations shown in Fig. 3(a). (c) Dependence of the drag reduction rate 𝜂on the number of birds. The inset highlights the cases corresponding to the configurations shown in Fig. 3(a).
FIG. 4. Stability diagram in the (𝑖, 𝜆) plane for a formation of 13 birds. The red vertical lines indicate the interval of 𝜆for which the formation remains stable.

FIG. 5. Steady configurations and drag reduction rates in inhomogeneous formations with 𝑁= 13. (a)–(c) Steady-state configurations. The coordinates are scaled by the reference wingspan 2𝑏and shifted so that the leader is located at the origin. The modified bird is located at (a) the outermost bird (𝑖= 1), (b) an intermediate bird on the left wing (𝑖= 4), and (c) the leader (𝑖= 7). In (a) and (b), the modified bird is highlighted by a black dashed box. Different colors correspond to 𝜆< 1, 𝜆= 1, and 𝜆> 1. (d)–(f) Corresponding individual drag reduction rates 𝜂𝑖.
